content.text: The Core Organizing Principle: Shared Lifecycle

The most reliable guide for grouping resources is shared lifecycle: resources that are created together, updated together, and deleted together belong in the same resource group. When you delete a resource group, every resource inside it is deleted. This is the mechanism, and your organizational structure should align with it.

A VM, its network interface, its managed disk, and its NSG all belong together — they form one deployable, deletable unit. A shared virtual network used by five applications should be in its own group so deleting one application's group does not take the network with it.

Create one resource group per application per environment. All resources for myapp in production go in myapp-prod; all dev resources go in myapp-dev. This is simple, makes cost isolation trivial, and keeps environment-level lifecycle operations (delete all dev resources) one command away.

# Environment pattern
az group create --name myapp-dev  --location eastus --tags environment=dev  application=myapp
az group create --name myapp-staging --location eastus --tags environment=staging application=myapp
az group create --name myapp-prod --location eastus --tags environment=prod application=myapp

# Delete entire dev environment in one command
az group delete --name myapp-dev --yes
Small applications with few shared resources
Excellent — one group per billing boundary
Simple — assign once per environment group
Hard when networking/shared infra spans environments

Each application or microservice gets its own resource group per environment. Teams own their group and manage RBAC independently. This scales to organizations with many teams, each responsible for one service.

# Workload pattern — multiple apps, multiple environments
for app in api worker frontend; do
  for env in dev prod; do
    az group create \
      --name "${app}-${env}" \
      --location eastus \
      --tags application="$app" environment="$env"
  done
done

# Resulting groups:
# api-dev, api-prod
# worker-dev, worker-prod
# frontend-dev, frontend-prod

Separate groups for infrastructure tiers. Shared networking infrastructure (VNets, NSGs, private DNS zones) lives in its own group with long lifetime. Compute groups (VMs, AKS, Functions) have a shorter lifecycle. Data groups (SQL, Cosmos DB, Storage) are often the most durable and owned by data teams.

APP=myapp
ENV=prod
LOCATION=eastus

az group create --name ${APP}-${ENV}-networking --location $LOCATION --tags tier=networking
az group create --name ${APP}-${ENV}-compute    --location $LOCATION --tags tier=compute
az group create --name ${APP}-${ENV}-data       --location $LOCATION --tags tier=data

# Networking group: VNet, subnets, NSGs, private DNS zones, Application Gateway
# Compute group:    AKS cluster, Function Apps, VMs, scale sets
# Data group:       SQL server, Cosmos DB, Storage accounts, Redis Cache

The most flexible approach combines environment and tier: {app}-{env}-{tier}. Dev environments often collapse all tiers into one group (myapp-dev) to reduce overhead; production environments split into separate tier groups for RBAC and lifecycle isolation.

# Combined pattern
APP=myapp
LOCATION=eastus

# Dev: single group (simple, easy to nuke)
az group create --name ${APP}-dev --location $LOCATION \
  --tags application=$APP environment=dev

# Staging: single group
az group create --name ${APP}-staging --location $LOCATION \
  --tags application=$APP environment=staging

# Prod: split by tier for isolation
for tier in networking compute data monitoring; do
  az group create --name ${APP}-prod-${tier} --location $LOCATION \
    --tags application=$APP environment=prod tier=$tier
done

Infrastructure shared across multiple applications — hub VNets, private DNS zones, Log Analytics workspaces, shared Key Vaults — should always live in dedicated resource groups separate from any application. This prevents accidental deletion when an application is decommissioned and makes it clear that the resource serves a platform-level function.
Azure Policy initiatives, custom role definitions

# Create platform (shared) resource groups
for name in platform-networking platform-monitoring platform-governance platform-secrets platform-containers platform-dns; do
  az group create \
    --name $name \
    --location eastus \
    --tags tier=platform owner=platform-team

  # Protect from deletion
  az lock create \
    --name protect-platform \
    --lock-type CanNotDelete \
    --resource-group $name
done
Tag Strategy That Complements RG Organization

Resource groups organize resources spatially (by container). Tags add orthogonal metadata that crosses group boundaries — enabling cost allocation, compliance queries, and operational filtering across the entire subscription.
IaC tool that created the resource
Date the resource should be reviewed/deleted

# Apply required tags to all resources in a group
RG=myapp-prod-compute
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list --resource-group $RG --query '[].id' -o tsv)

for id in $RESOURCE_IDS; do
  az tag update \
    --resource-id "$id" \
    --operation Merge \
    --tags application=myapp environment=prod team=backend
done

# Validate: find resources in the group missing the cost-center tag
az resource list \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --query "[?tags."cost-center"==null].{Name:name, Type:type}" \
  --output table

Management groups sit above subscriptions in the Azure hierarchy. While they don't directly organize resource groups, they affect which policies and RBAC assignments apply to groups inside their subscriptions. A well-designed management group hierarchy makes policy enforcement consistent across all resource groups.

# View the management group hierarchy
az account management-group list --output table

# List all subscriptions under a management group
az account management-group show \
  --name platform-dev \
  --expand \
  --recurse \
  --query 'children[].{Name:name, Id:id}' \
  --output table

# Assign a policy at management group scope (applies to all RGs in all child subscriptions)
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-cost-center-tag \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/platform \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025 \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "cost-center"}}'
Once you have a consistent naming and tagging convention, Azure Resource Graph enables subscription-wide and cross-subscription queries that enforce and audit your organizational structure.

# Inventory: all resource groups, their environments, and resource counts
az graph query -q '
ResourceContainers
| where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | summarize resourceCount=count() by resourceGroup
  ) on $left.name == $right.resourceGroup
| project name, location,
    environment=tostring(tags["environment"]),
    application=tostring(tags["application"]),
    tier=tostring(tags["tier"]),
    resourceCount=coalesce(resourceCount, 0)
| order by application asc, environment asc, tier asc
' --output table

# Find resource groups with no environment tag (untagged)
az graph query -q '
ResourceContainers
| where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
| where isnull(tags["environment"])
| project name, location, subscriptionId
' --output table
Enforcing Naming Convention with Azure Policy
One giant resource group for everything
Deleting any resource is risky; RBAC too broad; no lifecycle isolation
Split by environment + tier at minimum
Creates 20+ groups for one app; lifecycle becomes complex
Group by lifecycle unit, not by resource type
Enforce {app}-{env} pattern from day one via policy
Shared VNet in same group as ephemeral VMs — deletion risk
Shared infra always in dedicated platform groups
No tags (relying on group name alone)
Group name conveys limited info; cross-group queries impossible
Tag every group and enforce required tags with policy
Implementing an Organization Strategy from Scratch

Starting a new subscription gives you the opportunity to enforce structure from the beginning. The following end-to-end setup script creates a tiered organization for a multi-environment application, applies consistent tags, and assigns RBAC to team groups.

#!/bin/bash
# Full subscription bootstrap: naming + tags + RBAC + locks
set -euo pipefail

APP='myapp'
LOCATION='eastus'
SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Team Azure AD group object IDs (replace with real OIDs)
PLATFORM_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001'
DEV_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002'
OPS_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000003'

echo '--- Creating platform resource groups ---'
for name in platform-networking platform-monitoring platform-secrets; do
  az group create --name $name --location $LOCATION \
    --tags tier=platform owner=platform-team managed-by=script
  az lock create --name protect-platform --lock-type CanNotDelete \
    --resource-group $name
  az role assignment create --assignee $PLATFORM_OID --role Contributor \
    --resource-group $name
done

echo '--- Creating dev resource groups ---'
# Dev: single combined group for simplicity
az group create --name ${APP}-dev --location $LOCATION \
  --tags application=$APP environment=dev managed-by=script
az role assignment create --assignee $DEV_OID --role Contributor \
  --resource-group ${APP}-dev

echo '--- Creating staging resource groups ---'
az group create --name ${APP}-staging --location $LOCATION \
  --tags application=$APP environment=staging managed-by=script
az role assignment create --assignee $DEV_OID --role Contributor \
  --resource-group ${APP}-staging

echo '--- Creating prod resource groups ---'
for tier in networking compute data monitoring; do
  RG=${APP}-prod-${tier}
  az group create --name $RG --location $LOCATION \
    --tags application=$APP environment=prod tier=$tier managed-by=script
  az lock create --name protect-prod --lock-type CanNotDelete \
    --resource-group $RG
  az role assignment create --assignee $OPS_OID --role Contributor \
    --resource-group $RG
  # Dev team: read-only on prod
  az role assignment create --assignee $DEV_OID --role Reader \
    --resource-group $RG
done

echo 'Bootstrap complete.'

Resources in different groups often depend on each other — a VM in a compute group needs the VNet from the networking group, and an app in the compute group needs a storage account from the data group. Bicep handles cross-group references using the existing keyword and scope targeting.
Referencing a Resource in Another Group
Subscription-Scope Module That Deploys Across Multiple Groups
Organizations drift. New resources get created in wrong groups, naming conventions are violated, tags get omitted. Scheduled scripts or Azure Policy remediation tasks keep the structure clean.

#!/bin/bash
# Weekly RG compliance report
echo '=== Resource Group Compliance Report ==='
echo ''

echo '--- Groups missing environment tag ---'
az graph query -q '
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
  | where isnull(tags["environment"])
  | project name, location
' --output table

echo ''
echo '--- Groups missing application tag ---'
az graph query -q '
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
  | where isnull(tags["application"])
  | where not(name startswith "platform-")
  | project name, location
' --output table

echo ''
echo '--- Resources missing cost-center tag ---'
az graph query -q '
  Resources
  | where isnull(tags["cost-center"])
  | summarize count() by resourceGroup
  | order by count_ desc
  | take 20
' --output table

echo ''
echo '--- Prod groups without CanNotDelete lock ---'
az graph query -q '
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
  | where tags["environment"] == "prod"
  | join kind=leftouter (
      Resources
      | where type == "microsoft.authorization/locks"
      | where properties.level == "CanNotDelete"
      | project resourceGroup
    ) on resourceGroup
  | where isnull(resourceGroup1)
  | project name
' --output table
Auto-Remediate: Apply Missing Tags to Resources in a Group

import subprocess, json

def az(cmd):
    r = subprocess.run(['az'] + cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
    return json.loads(r.stdout) if r.stdout.strip() else None

def remediate_tags(rg_name):
    '''Copy missing RG tags to all resources in the group.'''
    rg = az(['group', 'show', '--name', rg_name])
    rg_tags = rg.get('tags', {})
    if not rg_tags:
        print(f'  {rg_name}: no tags on RG, skipping')
        return

    resources = az(['resource', 'list', '--resource-group', rg_name])
    for res in resources:
        res_tags = res.get('tags') or {}
        missing = {k: v for k, v in rg_tags.items() if k not in res_tags}
        if missing:
            tag_args = [f'{k}={v}' for k, v in missing.items()]
            subprocess.run(
                ['az', 'tag', 'update',
                 '--resource-id', res['id'],
                 '--operation', 'Merge',
                 '--tags'] + tag_args,
                check=True, capture_output=True
            )
            print(f'  Tagged {res["name"]}: {missing}')

# Run against all prod groups
groups = az(['group', 'list',
             '--query', '[?tags.environment==\'prod\'].name',
             '--output', 'json'])
for rg in groups:
    print(f'Remediating: {rg}')
    remediate_tags(rg)

Organize resource groups around shared lifecycle first, then add environment and tier boundaries where teams, cost allocation, or RBAC scope require it. Use the pattern {app}-{env}-{tier} for application groups and platform-{function} for shared infrastructure groups. Protect production and platform groups with CanNotDelete locks. Enforce naming conventions and required tags via Azure Policy from subscription creation. Azure Resource Graph provides the cross-group querying needed to audit that your organization stays consistent as the subscription grows.
